elements.sent-2411-001: In database applications involving sensitive data, the dual imperatives of data confidentiality and provable (verifiable) query processing are important.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper introduces \sysname, a database system that leverages non-interactive zero-knowledge proofs (ZKP) to support both confidentiality and provability.
elements.sent-2411-003: Unlike traditional databases, \sysname~ enhances confidentiality by ensuring that raw data remains exclusively with the host, while also enabling verifying the correctness of query responses by providing proofs to clients.
elements.sent-2411-004: The main innovation in this paper is proposing efficient ZKP designs (called circuits) for basic operations in SQL query processing.
elements.sent-2411-005: These basic operation circuits are then combined to form ZKP circuits for larger, more complex queries.
elements.sent-2411-006: We demonstrate the performance of \sysname~ with the standard TPC-H benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our experimental results show that \sysname~ can efficiently achieve both confidentiality and provability, outperforming existing state-of-the-art ZKP methods.
elements.sent-2411-008: As databases serve as the backbone of diverse applications, the responsibility entrusted to them extends beyond data storage---it encompasses the safeguarding of sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-009: This is an important consideration, especially for databases that store personal data.
elements.sent-2411-010: Also, in applications with sensitive information such as census data or unemployment statistics, it is important for users to trust that the database owner is using the correct database (i.e., one that is consistent with the claimed database) and that the provided answers accurately reflect the intended computations.
elements.sent-2411-011: We tackle the problem of providing a database solution to ensure two characteristics: (1)~Confidentiality: this property means that raw data is only maintained at the host (i.e., service provider) and is not shared or made public to any other node.
elements.sent-2411-012: Other nodes only receive responses to specific queries that they send to the host node and that the host node agrees to process.
elements.sent-2411-013: This means that the results computed by the host node and sent back to users reflect the correct processing of the query on the private database.
elements.sent-2411-014: This requires that the host node provides a proof of the correctness of the query response to the user.
elements.sent-2411-015: As an example, consider the healthcare sector, where medical research institutions collect patient data for collaborative studies.
elements.sent-2411-016: Institution X may wish to share insights about the data with data consumers Y, Z, and W without disclosing the raw data.
elements.sent-2411-017: Data consumers send queries to X, where queries are processed and their responses sent back to the consumer.
elements.sent-2411-018: X has control on what queries to answer and therefore can control the confidentiality of data~\cite{byun2008purpose .}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, because data consumers do not have access to the raw database, they cannot verify the correctness of processing.
elements.sent-2411-020: ZKP constitutes a powerful cryptographic tool that enables one party, the prover, to convince another party, the verifier, of the correctness of a statement without revealing any specific information about the statement itself.
elements.sent-2411-021: The protocols used in ZKP systems can be made interactive and non-interactive ZKP.
elements.sent-2411-022: Interactive ZKP utlize cryptographic protocols wherein two entities, namely a prover and a verifier, dynamically exchange messages to establish the validity of a statement without revealing any sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-023: Previous research has introduced interactive ZKP for verifying SQL queries~\cite{zhang2015integridb, zhang2017vsql, li2023zksql .}
elements.sent-2411-024: The rationale behind using interactive ZKP for SQL query verification (like ZKSQL~\cite{li2023zksql ) is that they enable the prover to engage with the verifier in multiple rounds, incrementally constructing and verifying parts of the proof.}
elements.sent-2411-025: This step-by-step interaction often results in smaller circuit sizes, as the prover can break down the computation into manageable parts rather than generating a single large, complex proof upfront.
elements.sent-2411-026: However, the interactive nature introduces challenges related to synchronization and availability between the prover and verifier.
elements.sent-2411-027: Specifically, interactive ZKP are accompanied by two main drawbacks that currently limit their widespread adoption in practical applications: (1)~They necessitate interaction between the prover and verifier throughout the proof protocol, imposing requirements for availability and synchronization between the involved parties.
elements.sent-2411-028: This challenge exacerbates failures and timeouts.
elements.sent-2411-029: This excludes the possibility of reusing (and caching) previously computed responses and proofs for multiple verifiers.
elements.sent-2411-030: Unlike interactive ZKP, non-interactive ZKP empowers a prover to generate a single, self-contained proof that can be subsequently verified by any party possessing the necessary verification key.
elements.sent-2411-031: This characteristic eliminates the requirement for real-time interaction during the proof protocol, providing greater flexibility and efficiency in scenarios where asynchronous verification or limited interactivity is desired.
elements.sent-2411-032: Non-interactive ZKP, however, can experience significant overheads if the underlying cryptographic circuits are not carefully optimized.
elements.sent-2411-033: In this paper, we propose a database system named \sysname, which incorporates non-interactive ZKP and the recursive proof composition technique.
elements.sent-2411-034: These circuits enable ZKP frameworks to generate proofs of correctness for computations.
elements.sent-2411-035: In \sysname, we design circuits to represent basic query operations such as range checks, sorting, group-by, and joins, which are combined to handle more complex queries.
elements.sent-2411-036: Circuit design directly impacts proof generation performance.
elements.sent-2411-037: We optimize \sysname's circuits using the PLONKish framework~\cite{plonkish , considering factors such as circuit size, polynomial degree, and batching construction.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Additionally, {\sysname leverages a recursive structure for composing proofs of multiple statements, reducing the overall proof size and computational overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-039: This is made possible by advancements in proving systems that utilize recursive proof composition techniques~\cite{bowe2019recursive, kothapalli2022nova .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Table~\ref{table:method_comparison compares \sysname~and prior research on verifiable database systems~\cite{zhang2015integridb, zhang2017vsql, li2023zksql in terms of three properties: (1) the assurance of zero-knowledge (i.e., confidentiality), (2) non-interactive operability, and (3) applicability to arbitrary SQL queries.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The protocols used in vSQL and vSQL+ are originally presented in an interactive manner, however they can be made non-interactive via the Fiat-Shamir heuristic~\cite{fiat1986prove .}
elements.sent-2411-042: To the best of our knowledge, {\sysname is the first system that achieves all these desirable properties.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Also, in the evaluation section, we show that {\sysname performance outperforms the state-of-the-art ZKP methods.}
elements.sent-2411-044: This paper is structured as follows: Section~\ref{sec:background presents background material.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Section~\ref{sec:workflow proposes the general design of {\sysname followed by the detailed design in Section~\ref{sec:gates .}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Section~\ref{sec:related_work presents related work and Section~\ref{sec:conclusion concludes the paper.}}

elements.sent-2411-048: In the area of ZKP, a prover can convincingly demonstrate the truth of a given statement to a verifier without divulging any additional information.
elements.sent-2411-049: Specifically, ZKP empowers a prover $\mathcal{P}$ with a private witness $\mathnormal{w}$ (a ``private witness" refers to a piece of secret information such as a secret input, e.g., database) to validate the truth of a public statement $\mathcal{F}$ (with respect to $\mathnormal{w}$) to a verifier $\mathcal{V}$, while preserving the confidentiality of the underlying information $w$.
elements.sent-2411-050: For instance, suppose a database contains information about employees, and the data owner wants to prove to the verifier that the average salary of the employees is a certain number without disclosing individual salary details.
elements.sent-2411-051: In this scenario, the private witness $\mathnormal{w}$ is the information about individual salaries, and the public statement $\mathcal{F}$ is the average salary computed by the SQL query.
elements.sent-2411-052: In scenarios with multiple verifiers, such as healthcare settings where various institutions need to verify query results, interactive protocols can become impractical due to the need for multiple rounds of communication with each verifier.
elements.sent-2411-053: Non-interactive ZKP addresses this issue by allowing the prover to generate a single proof that can be verified by all parties without further interaction.
elements.sent-2411-054: For public-coin interactive protocols~\cite{ZK-origins , the Fiat-Shamir heuristic~\cite{fiat1986prove effectively converts them into non-interactive ones (e.g., vSQL), maintaining efficiency and minimizing computational overhead while supporting asynchronous verification.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The use of arithmetic circuits is the most common paradigm for expressing computations within ZKP systems.
elements.sent-2411-056: We introduce the PLONKish arithmetization~\cite{plonkish that we use in \sysname.}
elements.sent-2411-057: We emphasize that the PLONKish circuits used in PoneglyphDB serve primarily as a vehicle to demonstrate the potential of non-interactive ZK proofs within database management systems (DBMSs), rather than representing a state-of-the-art protocol.
elements.sent-2411-058: PLONKish circuits are defined in terms of a rectangular matrix of values.
elements.sent-2411-059: We refer to rows, columns, and cells of this matrix with the conventional meanings.
elements.sent-2411-060: In the following, we introduce the key definitions of PLONKish circuits that are relevant to our circuit optimization.
elements.sent-2411-061: Fixed columns are fixed by the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-062: The values in the fixed columns are usually constants.
elements.sent-2411-063: Advice columns correspond to witness values.
elements.sent-2411-064: These are private inputs and intermediate values generated during the circuit computation.
elements.sent-2411-065: Instance columns are used for any elements shared between the prover and verifier.
elements.sent-2411-066: In most cases, they are used for public inputs and outputs.
elements.sent-2411-067: Equality constraints specify that two given cells must have equal values.
elements.sent-2411-068: For each row in the matrix, the multivariate polynomials over the field $\mathbb{F}$ must be evaluated to zero.
elements.sent-2411-069: Figure~\ref{fig:plonkish illustrates the PLONKish circuit for calculating the function $f(x, y, z) = 3 * (x + y) * z$.}
elements.sent-2411-070: The circuit utilizes three advice columns---advice1, advice2, and advice3---to store the private inputs and intermediate values during computation.
elements.sent-2411-071: In row 0, the values for $x$ and $y$ are put into the cells in advice1 and advice2, respectively (i.e. $x = a1$ and $y = b1$).
elements.sent-2411-072: A polynomial constraint is introduced to ensure that $c1 = a1 + b1$, calculating $x + y$.
elements.sent-2411-073: Next in row 1, the value of the input $z$ (i.e. $z=a2$) is put into the cell in advice1.
elements.sent-2411-074: Since $a1 + b1$ was computed as $c1$, its value is propagated to $b2$ and an equality constraint sets $b2 = c1$.
elements.sent-2411-075: A multiplier gate (with polynomial constraints) then calculates $c2 = a2 * b2$, evaluating $a2*b2-c2 = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Finally in row 2, the constant $3$ is brought into advice1 at the cell of $a3$ and fixed by the copy (or equality) constraint $a3 = 3$.
elements.sent-2411-077: The previous intermediate result $(a1 + b1)*a2 = c2$ is copied into $b3$, with an equality constraint $b3 = c2$.
elements.sent-2411-078: The final output cell $c3$ calculates $a3 * b3$, which evaluates to $3 * (a1 + b1) * a2$.
elements.sent-2411-079: This result is copied into the public instance column at $o1$, allowing the verifier to read off the final output.
elements.sent-2411-080: Through a series of advice columns, equality constraints, and polynomial operations, the circuit computes the desired function in a modular fashion while keeping intermediate values private.
elements.sent-2411-081: The verifier only sees the final outputs revealed in the instance column.
elements.sent-2411-082: To facilitate the modularity of PLONKish circuits, the designs of basic functions are represented as gates.
elements.sent-2411-083: A gate is a collection of columns and constraints that together implement a basic operation, such as division and multiplication.
elements.sent-2411-084: In our case, we will define gates for basic operations for query processing such as range checks and sorting.
elements.sent-2411-085: Gates can then be combined to implement more complex functions (or more complex gates).

elements.sent-2411-086: In \sysname, the prover $\mathcal{P}$ hosts a copy of a private database $\mathcal{DB}$.
elements.sent-2411-087: The prover receives queries from one or more verifiers, collectively denoted $\mathcal{V}$.
elements.sent-2411-088: The prover does not provide raw access to the data in the database.
elements.sent-2411-089: However, it answers queries sent by the verifiers.
elements.sent-2411-090: The prover, holding exclusive access to this private database, is tasked with executing the queries.
elements.sent-2411-091: These circuits, delineated by gates and polynomial constraints, performs the computations that need to be proven.
elements.sent-2411-092: The SQL circuit encodes the desired SQL logic to be evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-093: This circuit allows for the computation of different inputs provided by the verifiers during the verification phase.
elements.sent-2411-094: A database commitment is a cryptographic representation of a database state, enabling proof of properties about the data without revealing the data itself.
elements.sent-2411-095: This allows the prover to include evidence in the proof that the query was indeed processed on $\mathcal{DB}$.
elements.sent-2411-096: We employ the Inner Product Argument (IPA) protocol~\cite{bootle2016efficient , operating over a 254-bit prime field, to generate this commitment.}
elements.sent-2411-097: We choose the IPA protocol for the following reasons: (1) the proving time is typically linear with respect to the circuit size or the degree of the committed polynomial, (2) the proof size and verification time are logarithmic in the circuit size, due to the recursive structure of the inner product proof~\cite{bowe2019recursive , and (3) it is compatible with PLONKish-based circuit designs.}
elements.sent-2411-098: By leveraging IPA, we can encode both the database commitment and the desired SQL logic within a unified framework~\cite{bootle2016efficient, bunz2018bulletproofs .}
elements.sent-2411-099: The public parameters are a shared foundation for both provers and verifiers in proof creation and validation.
elements.sent-2411-100: This process utilizes publicly verifiable randomness and avoids the need for a trusted setup~\cite{chiesa2020marlin .}
elements.sent-2411-101: Initial proof creation uses only public information, forming the basis for subsequent proofs~\cite{bowe2019recursive .}
elements.sent-2411-102: This key is used to generate proofs corresponding to the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-103: Concurrently, a verification key is also produced, grounded in the same public parameters and circuit description.
elements.sent-2411-104: This verification key empowers verifiers to authenticate the proofs associated with this circuit.
elements.sent-2411-105: Leveraging the previously established database commitment, the prover creates a verifiable link between the committed database, query execution, and result.
elements.sent-2411-106: This process incorporates commitments to relevant database rows and intermediate computation steps, forming a chain of verifiable commitments from the initial database state to the final query output.
elements.sent-2411-107: The verification algorithm essentially interpolates the wiring polynomials and checks that all constraints are satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-108: In {\sysname , we utilize existing work from the cryptography community to perform the steps of key and proof generation and verification.}
elements.sent-2411-109: The main contribution of our work is in the design of SQL circuits that allow for efficient proving and verification given the utilized framework~\cite{halo2 .}
elements.sent-2411-110: In the PoneglyphDB framework, the core parties involved are the database owner, acting as the prover, and the clients, acting as the verifiers.
elements.sent-2411-111: The prover and verifiers do not trust each other as we detail below.
elements.sent-2411-112: There is an additional entity, the auditor, that both the prover and verifier trust (there can be multiple auditors).
elements.sent-2411-113: The following are trust concerns between the parties:.
elements.sent-2411-114: For example, a server responsible for running SQL queries might use a tampered or fake database to process a query and provide incorrect results to the verifier.
elements.sent-2411-115: The server could mistakenly or intentionally alter or fabricate the query results.
elements.sent-2411-116: The client could craft a series of queries to uncover sensitive patterns, private data, or proprietary information, breaching the prover's confidentiality.
elements.sent-2411-117: We now map this trust model to a real-world scenario.
elements.sent-2411-118: In general, this trust model applies to cases when a database owner has sensitive data and wants to enable other entities (clients) to query the database without revealing data beyond what the database owner allows.
elements.sent-2411-119: In the healthcare scenario, a hospital H wants to provide query access to its database of patient data.
elements.sent-2411-120: It does not want to reveal all data, but would allow answering specific types of queries.
elements.sent-2411-121: The clients can be healthcare providers or research institutions that want to utilize H’s database for their research.
elements.sent-2411-122: In this scenario, an auditor can be a government or regulatory entity that both H and clients trust.
elements.sent-2411-123: Next, we map the trust model assumptions onto this example:.
elements.sent-2411-124: The clients do not trust the hospital H to be using a correct database of patient’s data: The hospital H does not reveal the raw database to the clients, and therefore clients cannot attest to the authenticity of the database used to answer queries.
elements.sent-2411-125: The clients do not trust the hospital H to process queries on the database correctly: The hospital H may process the queries on the database incorrectly (e.g., by using approximation techniques to save costs, or by completely fabricating results).
elements.sent-2411-126: The clients may attempt to leak data from query responses: The clients may want to know more information about the database of H than what is provided in the answer to the query.
elements.sent-2411-127: To address these trust issues, PoneglyphDB introduces the following measures: (1) Cryptographic Commitment to the Database: PoneglyphDB requires the prover to make an irrevocable cryptographic commitment to the database.
elements.sent-2411-128: This ensures that the prover cannot substitute the real database with a bogus one while still producing valid query results.
elements.sent-2411-129: The commitment is made public and shared in an irrevocable and immutable manner, e.g., by utilizing a decentralized blockchain such as Ethereum.
elements.sent-2411-130: This ensures that the prover will use the same database that corresponds to the database commitment as clients have access to an irrevocable, immutable database commitment that they can compare with the commitment used in the received proof.
elements.sent-2411-131: The database commitment can also be audited by a third-party auditor (e.g., a regulatory or government entity trusted by both the prover and verifiers).
elements.sent-2411-132: The auditor in this case reads the raw database from the database owner, verifies its authenticity, and validates that the database commitment of the authentic database corresponds to the commitment that is shared in the blockchain and accessible by the verifiers.
elements.sent-2411-133: To prove the correctness of database updates and generate new commitments, a naive method would be to recompute the commitment for the entire database after each update.
elements.sent-2411-134: However, this approach is inefficient for large databases.
elements.sent-2411-135: A more advanced method might involve using a Merkle tree structure~\cite{DBLP:conf/sp/Merkle80 , where only the affected subtree is updated and recomputed, enabling efficient localized proof generation.}
elements.sent-2411-136: Another potential method involves leveraging batch update techniques, where multiple updates are aggregated into a single proof.
elements.sent-2411-137: This process ensures the prover cannot return fabricated results.
elements.sent-2411-138: These proofs guarantee that the client only receives the query result without extracting or inferring any additional information about the underlying database.
elements.sent-2411-139: The zero-knowledge property protects the database from unwanted data leakage beyond what is revealed from the query response.
elements.sent-2411-140: This zero-knowledge property can be combined with other techniques that prevent other types of leakage of private data.
elements.sent-2411-141: For this reason, it is important to distinguish between the types of leakage that are prevented or allowed by the zero-knowledge property and complement it with other techniques.
elements.sent-2411-142: A ZKP does not reveal information beyond the query response, but it does reveal what is part of the query response (and anything that can be implied by the query response).
elements.sent-2411-143: Therefore, if there is data that a user should not have access to but is part of the query response, then it is leaked to the client.
elements.sent-2411-144: To prevent this type of leakage, ZKP can be combined with techniques such as access control, data masking, query filtering, and policy management to prevent processing queries on data that the user would not have access to ~\cite{agrawal2002hippocratic .}
elements.sent-2411-145: These techniques, typically applied as pre-processing steps, allow the prover to decide whether a query should be processed.
elements.sent-2411-146: For example, if the client’s query asks to get raw data that should not be revealed, then the prover would not process the query.
elements.sent-2411-147: Query filtering, in particular, helps prevent leakage by modifying the query plan to exclude sensitive information before execution, ensuring that only authorized data is included in the response.
elements.sent-2411-148: Another type of leakage is to infer information about the database that were not intended to be revealed directly in the query response.
elements.sent-2411-149: This type of leakage can be prevented by incorporating differential privacy techniques as we discuss at the end of Section~\ref{subsec:security .}
elements.sent-2411-150: With these techniques, and reflecting back to the mapping to the real-world scenario above, we observe the following: (1) PoneglyphDB ensures that the hospital H uses an authentic database to process queries.
elements.sent-2411-151: This is by utilizing a trusted third-party auditor that verifies that the publicly shared irrevocable and immutable database commitment corresponds to an authentic database.
elements.sent-2411-152: If a prover attempts to utilize a different database, then the client is able to discover this as the client would match the publicly shared database commitment that is authenticated by an auditor with the database commitment that is used to process the query and received as part of the response.
elements.sent-2411-153: Hospital H can integrate further techniques---such as differential privacy---to ensure that other types of data leakage would not occur (as we describe in Section~\ref{subsec:security ).}
elements.sent-2411-154: If the prover can generate the PLONKish circuit (including the output) of a query, it can always convince the verifier that the PLONKish circuit of the query is true.
elements.sent-2411-155: For any false PLONKish circuit (including any wrong witness, inputs and output), the probability of a dishonest prover successfully convincing an honest verifier is negligible.
elements.sent-2411-156: When the verifier is convinced the PLONKish circuit is correct, the prover actually possesses a valid witness.
elements.sent-2411-157: The verifier learns only the information that can be inferred from the structure of the PLONKish circuit and the output of the query.
elements.sent-2411-158: No additional knowledge about the private witnesses or the database is revealed.
elements.sent-2411-159: Figure~\ref{fig:system illustrates the detailed components involved in generating ZKP within \sysname.}
elements.sent-2411-160: The system comprises two main parts: the PLONKish circuit and the Halo2 proving system.
elements.sent-2411-161: The PLONKish circuit serves as the input to the Halo2 proving system, which then generates ZKP for the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-162: Our primary contribution lies in the design of the PLONKish circuits, which are tailored to optimize the performance and efficiency of the ZKP generation process.
elements.sent-2411-163: The PLONKish circuit represents the computation of a SQL query.
elements.sent-2411-164: This circuit is a mathematical representation of the logical operations and constraints involved in the SQL query execution.
elements.sent-2411-165: The prover, who possesses the private database, assigns values to all circuit variables based on the actual data from the database.
elements.sent-2411-166: This step involves mapping the data inputs to the corresponding variables in the circuit, ensuring that the computation is correctly set up for proof generation.
elements.sent-2411-167: The prover must use the database agreed upon with the verifier by utilizing the previously established database commitment.
elements.sent-2411-168: The {\it Polynomial representation component, provided by the Halo2 proving system, translates the circuit into a polynomial form.}
elements.sent-2411-169: This component encodes the computation and its constraints as polynomial equations, making them suitable for ZKP.
elements.sent-2411-170: The {\it Polynomial commitment component, also provided by the Halo2 proving system, allows the prover to commit to the polynomial evaluations without revealing the actual polynomials.}
elements.sent-2411-171: This ensures that the prover cannot alter the polynomials after the commitment, maintaining the integrity of the proof.
elements.sent-2411-172: The {\it Halo2 proving system takes the committed polynomials and generates opening proofs.}
elements.sent-2411-173: These proofs are designed to show that the committed polynomials satisfy the polynomial constraints derived from the PLONKish circuit.
elements.sent-2411-174: It is important to note that the PLONKish circuits implemented in \sysname~ are primarily intended to illustrate the feasibility of using non-interactive ZK proofs within DBMSs, rather than to claim they represent the latest advancements in ZK protocol design.

elements.sent-2411-175: The correctness and security of \sysname~ depend on PLONKish circuits and the Halo2 proving system.
elements.sent-2411-176: Regarding the security of the Halo2 proving system used in \sysname~, the prover and verifier engage in a non-interactive ZKP protocol utilizing Halo2's polynomial commitment scheme and recursive proof composition.
elements.sent-2411-177: The detailed security analysis of this protocol has been rigorously established in ~\cite{halo2-protocol, bowe2019recursive .}
elements.sent-2411-178: We present a high-level summary of the correctness of the system below.
elements.sent-2411-179: To analyze the cryptographic protocols employed in Halo2, we utilize the Algebraic Group Model (AGM)~\cite{fuchsbauer2018algebraic .}
elements.sent-2411-180: The AGM is used to analyze protocols that rely on discrete logarithm assumptions in prime-order groups, a fundamental aspect of Halo2's design.
elements.sent-2411-181: The AGM evaluates the security of cryptographic protocols by requiring that adversaries must explicitly compute group elements from previously observed elements, emphasizing discrete logarithm-type assumptions in prime-order groups.
elements.sent-2411-182: The proving logic of the circuits in \sysname~ is designed to be oblivious.
elements.sent-2411-183: This means that the execution of the proving algorithm does not depend on the specific values of the private inputs (the witness).
elements.sent-2411-184: Instead, the prover performs the same operations regardless of the actual witness values, which helps ensure that no information about the private inputs is leaked through the proof generation process or the resulting proof itself.
elements.sent-2411-185: For example, when implementing a sorting algorithm, the circuit would compare and swap elements in a fixed pattern regardless of their actual values, ensuring the same operations are performed for any input.
elements.sent-2411-186: Similarly, for conditional statements, both branches of the condition are typically evaluated, and their results are combined using a selector value, rather than following only one branch based on the private condition.
elements.sent-2411-187: To protect the privacy of table cardinalities and intermediate result sizes in join operations, we adopt the method introduced in ZKSQL~\cite{li2023zksql .}
elements.sent-2411-188: This approach involves introducing dummy tuples into our query evaluation process, effectively obscuring true data sizes while maintaining consistent row counts throughout query execution.
elements.sent-2411-189: Since the proof inherently includes the result of the query, a client could, for example, issue a query like "SELECT * FROM T" to attempt to retrieve all values from T, which threatens data privacy.
elements.sent-2411-190: Beyond the direct data exposure of returned results, sensitive data can also be exposed indirectly.
elements.sent-2411-191: Specifically, when data points or records in the database are correlated, query results may still leak sensitive information about records that are not part of the response.
elements.sent-2411-192: For instance, correlation among records---such as similar attributes shared across groups or statistical dependencies---can allow a client to infer additional, sensitive details beyond the returned query results.
elements.sent-2411-193: In this way, even without direct access to records that are not included in query results, clients can potentially exploit patterns in the query response to gain insights into the broader dataset indirectly.
elements.sent-2411-194: This poses a data privacy risk that needs to be handled carefully.
elements.sent-2411-195: In this work, we do not incorporate differential privacy and leave it as an avenue of future work.
elements.sent-2411-196: We note, however, that methods of incorporating differential privacy to ZKP systems such as {\sysname would lead to additional overhead in the circuit design.. \end{sloppypar.}}
elements.sent-2411-197: In this section, we present customized gates to represent SQL queries arithmetization in circuits.
elements.sent-2411-198: Our goal is to introduce efficient designs by creating gates that have low-degree polynomials and a smaller number of circuit constraints.
elements.sent-2411-199: We design with low-degree polynomials because ZKP relies on cryptographic primitives where evaluating higher-degree polynomials is computationally expensive.
elements.sent-2411-200: We first introduce a range check gate as it is involved in many SQL operations like {\bf ``filter'' , {\bf ``sort'' , {\bf ``group by'' and {\bf ``join'' .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-201: Consider the range check statement $x \leq t$, where $x$ is a private value and $t$ is the public query input.
elements.sent-2411-202: A naive encoding compares $x$ against each possible value using the polynomial equation: $\prod_{i=0 ^{t (x-i) = 0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-203: The degree of this polynomial grows linearly with $t$, making proof generation and verification computationally infeasible for large $t$.
elements.sent-2411-204: In this work, we leverage a lookup table~\cite{gabizon2020plookup circuit structure to design the range check gate.}
elements.sent-2411-205: The intuition behind using lookup tables lies in the idea of precomputing and storing results for a range of possible inputs.
elements.sent-2411-206: Our work builds upon the widely used Plookup framework~\cite{gabizon2020plookup , which is well-established in the ZKP domain.}
elements.sent-2411-207: While there are alternatives, such as Jolt~\cite{setty2024unlocking , we leave exploration of these methods for future work.}
elements.sent-2411-208: Instead of reinventing cryptographic primitives, we focus on solving concrete implementation challenges to enhance efficiency of SQL operations within the PLONK framework.
elements.sent-2411-209: While previous works~\cite{zhang2017vsql, li2023zksql implement operations like filter, sort, and join using boolean or logic gate frameworks, our approach utilizes arithmetic-based PLONKish circuits.}
elements.sent-2411-210: This fundamental difference introduces significant challenges, as arithmetic circuits require different optimization strategies compared to boolean circuits.
elements.sent-2411-211: Adapting SQL operators in this context necessitates a rethinking of their expression and optimization to achieve maximum efficiency.. Definition.
elements.sent-2411-212: The input to a range check gate, which checks if elements are between \( y_1 \) and \( y_m \), consists of a column \( C_{\text{in \) containing \( n \) elements \( \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\} \) to be range-checked, and a column (lookup table) \( T \) containing \( m \) sorted values \( \{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_m\} \) representing the valid range, where \( y_1 < y_2 < \cdots < y_m \).}}
elements.sent-2411-213: The output of the range check gate is a column \( C_{\text{out \) containing \( n \) elements \( \{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_n\} \), where each \( z_i \) indicates whether \( x_i \) is within the range defined by the lookup table \( T \).}}
elements.sent-2411-214: Specifically, \( z_i = 1 \) if \( x_i \in \{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_m\} \), and \( z_i = 0 \) otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-215: Design A: A single range check.
elements.sent-2411-216: We start with a simple case where we only want to prove that a single value $x$ is in a specific range $[0,t]$, hence proving that $0 \leq x \leq t$.
elements.sent-2411-217: In the first step of constructing the circuit, we create a private array $P$ (stored in an advice column) with the same length of set $Q$ where the first element in $P$ is $x$ and the other elements are any values copied from $Q$ (these values can be duplicates).
elements.sent-2411-218: Then, the prover supplies a permutation of $P$, denoted by $P'$ where $P'$ is private and stored in an advice column in the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-219: The values in $P'$ are sorted so that duplicate values are row-adjacent to each other.
elements.sent-2411-220: In the second step, we establish a fixed column to store the set $Q$, arranging the values in ascending order.
elements.sent-2411-221: This organization ensures that both the prover and the verifier are aware of the values and their corresponding indices within the table.
elements.sent-2411-222: Such knowledge is crucial for determining the size of the lookup table needed.
elements.sent-2411-223: The prover supplies a permutation of $Q$, denoted by $Q'$, where $Q'$ is private and stored in an advice column in the PLONKish circuit.
elements.sent-2411-224: The purpose of the permutation $Q'$ is to hide the position of values in $Q$ as the verifier knows all the information about $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-225: Then, the circuit can compare values in $Q'$ and $P'$ to check whether $x$ (in $P'$) is equal to some value in $Q'$, without revealing which value is that in $Q'$.
elements.sent-2411-226: Now, we show how we can check whether the value $x$ in $P'$ is equal to a value in $Q'$.
elements.sent-2411-227: The values in $Q'$ are arranged in a specific order such that either $P'_i = Q'_i$ or that $P'_i = P'_{i-1}$ where $P'_i$ and $Q'_i$ represent the $i$-th elements of $P'$ and $Q'$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-228: Specifically, we enforce that the first values of $P$ and $Q$ are equal, i.e, $P'_i = Q'_i$ with $i=0$.
elements.sent-2411-229: If $P'_i \neq Q'_i$ for $i>0$, we enforce that $P'_i = P'_{i-1}$ meaning that $P'_i$ must be a duplicate of $P'_{i-1}$ in this case.
elements.sent-2411-230: Therefore, these constraints guarantee that every value in $P'_i$ is equal to some value in $Q'$.
elements.sent-2411-231: Formally, we enforce that either $P'_i = Q'_i$ or that $P'_i = P'_{i-1}$, using the rule: \begin{equation}\label{eq:c1} 0 = \begin{cases} (P'_i - Q'_i) \cdot (P'_i - P'_{i-1}) \text{if } 1 \leq i \leq len(Q')-1, ~P'_i - Q'_i \text{if } i = 0. \end{cases}\end{equation} With the above polynomial constraints, the verifier knows that all the values of $P'$ are in $Q'$ without knowing the position information of the values in $P'$ and $Q'$ (therefore it does not know the exact values at each position in $P'$, preserving the privacy of the $x$ value).
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq:c1} 0 =  (P'_i - Q'_i) \cdot (P'_i - P'_{i-1}) if  1 \leq i \leq len(Q')-1, ~P'_i - Q'_i if  i = 0. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-232: Since $P'$ and $Q'$ are permutations of $P$ and $Q$, the polynomial constraints above ensure that all the values of $P$ (and $P'$) are in $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-233: To ensure this property, we develop polynomial constraints to ensure that both $P'$ is a permutation of $P$ and $Q'$ is a permutation of $Q$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:permutation} \prod_{i=0}^{len(Q)-1} (P_i + \alpha) (Q_i + \beta) = \prod_{i=0}^{len(Q)-1} (P'_i + \alpha) (Q'_i + \beta)\end{equation} where $P_i$, $Q_i$, $P'_i$ and $Q'_i$ represent the $i-$th element in $P$, $Q$, $P'$, and $Q'$ respectively, and $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are randomly chosen parameters.
elements.sent-2411-234: The random values \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) conceal the contents of the columns, ensuring confidentiality during verification and preventing zero products from causing collisions due to poor randomness.
elements.sent-2411-235: To make the circuit formulation efficient, we express it as a recursive function to ensure that each equation maintains a low polynomial degree: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \label{eq:permu1} Z_{i+1} = Z_{i} \cdot \frac{(P_i + \alpha)(Q_i + \beta)}{(P'_i+\alpha)(Q'_i + \beta)} Z_{\text{len}(Q)} = Z_0 = 1 \end{gathered}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-236: Figure~\ref{fig:lookup illustrates our proposed lookup table circuit design for proving a range check statement without revealing the specific values of $x_1$ and $x_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-237: The prover uses a fixed column \( Q \) accessible to verifiers, containing values in the range \([0, 4)\).
elements.sent-2411-238: An advice column \( P \) includes the actual values of \( x_1 \) and \( x_2 \) with the remaining cells filled arbitrarily from \( Q \).
elements.sent-2411-239: The prover then generates advice columns \( P' \) and \( Q' \) as permutations of \( P \) and \( Q \), respectively, with \( P' \) sorted in ascending order and duplicates adjacent, ensuring \( Q' \) starts with the same value as \( P' \).
elements.sent-2411-240: The prover checks that each \( P'_i \) is either equal to \( Q'_i \) or \( P'_{i-1 \).}
elements.sent-2411-241: This polynomial constraint ensures that values are within the range.

elements.sent-2411-243: The lookup table technique facilitates verifying whether all elements of an array \(P\) belong to the set \(Q = \{i \mid 0 \leq i \leq t, i\in \mathbb{Z}\}\), effectively checking if they fall within the range \([0, t]\).
elements.sent-2411-244: By organizing the elements of \(P\) into a single advice column and aligning the set \(Q\) within a fixed column, the lookup table approach previously discussed can be applied.
elements.sent-2411-245: For instance, the circuit depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:lookup can demonstrate whether the elements in the array \([0,2,1,3]\) (column \(Q'\)) are contained within the range \([0, 4)\) (column \(Q\)).}
elements.sent-2411-246: The complexity of verifying the inclusion of the arrays \([0]\) and \([0,1,2,3]\) within the range \([0, 4)\) remains consistent, as it necessitates the application of formulas~\ref{eq:c1 and \ref{eq:permu1 for each row.}}
elements.sent-2411-247: To mitigate the scalability concerns associated with range checks, especially when the size of the set \(Q\) becomes significantly large (e.g., \(2^{64}\)), we propose an optimization technique that leverages bitwise decomposition in conjunction with lookup tables.
elements.sent-2411-248: This method entails representing integers in a fixed bit-length format, such as 64 bits, and subsequently verifying the integrity of this bitwise representation in relation to the original integer value.
elements.sent-2411-249: Given an integer \(N\) and its representation as a sequence of bits \(b_0, b_1, \ldots, b_{k-1}\), where \(b_0\) is the least significant bit and \(b_{k-1}\) the most significant, the relationship between \(N\) and its bits is described by $N = \sum_{i=0 ^{k-1 b_i \cdot 2^i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-250: We partition the bitwise representation of integer \(N\) into smaller segments of 8 bits, referred to as u8 cell.
elements.sent-2411-251: This approach is predicated on the standard binary representation of integers, wherein the integer is segmented into 8-bit blocks.
elements.sent-2411-252: For instance, a 64-bit integer is divided into 8 u8 cells, each encapsulating an 8-bit slice of the integer.
elements.sent-2411-253: Constraints are imposed to ensure this decomposition is accurately executed with the constraints $N = \sum_{i=0 ^{7 c_i \cdot 2^{8i}$ for a 64-bit integer $N$, where $c_i$ represents the 8-bit segment of $N$ at position $i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-254: Each u8 cell $c_i$ should have values within the range $0$ to $255$ (inclusive), corresponding to $2^8 - 1$.
elements.sent-2411-255: To validate each u8 cell, we utilize a fixed-sized lookup table of size 256, which contains integers from $0$ to $255$.
elements.sent-2411-256: This table allows efficient range checking for each segment of the integer.
elements.sent-2411-257: The advantage of using this lookup table is that it is fixed-sized (256 entries) and can be reused multiple times for each u8 cell check.
elements.sent-2411-258: This ensures that each u8 cell $c_i$ can quickly verify if its value falls within the allowed range of \(0\) to \(2^8 - 1\).
elements.sent-2411-259: By reusing the lookup table across all 8 u8 cells of $N$, we streamline the validation process and ensure consistent range checks.
elements.sent-2411-260: The previous range check method faces limitations when a value falls outside the lookup range, making proof construction difficult.
elements.sent-2411-261: To this end, we introduce an augmented method that seamlessly integrates with lookup tables to facilitate range checks while gracefully handling conditional scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-262: When dealing with data filtering, an upper-bound value, denoted as $u$, typically exists.
elements.sent-2411-263: Consequently, for \(x \geq t\), this also implies \(u > x \geq t\).
elements.sent-2411-264: Therefore, proving \(u > x \geq t\) is equivalent to proving $0 \leq x - t \leq u$.
elements.sent-2411-265: To establish \(x < t\), it suffices to demonstrate that \(x - t < 0\).

elements.sent-2411-267: Introducing a binary variable, denoted as $check$, to determine whether \(x < t\), we ultimately prove the following statement: \begin{equation}\label{eq:lessthan2} 0 \leq (x - t) + \text{{check}} \cdot u < \text{{u}}\end{equation} To accomplish this, the prover configures several supporting columns in the PLONKish circuits.

elements.sent-2411-274: These constraints guarantee that the discrepancies between corresponding elements in the ``x'' and ``t'' columns align with the values stored in the ``x-t'' column.. Correctness.

elements.sent-2411-307: The first step in proving sort operations entails generating a witness, which includes the result of applying a specific sorting algorithm to the input data.

elements.sent-2411-330: In this section, we outline our methodology for verifying the correctness of group-by operations.. Definition.
elements.sent-2411-331: The input of a group-by gate consists of a table \( D \) with \( n \) columns \( C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n \), where each column represents different attributes of \( D \).
elements.sent-2411-332: The output is a table \( SD \) with \( n + 2 \) columns that rearranges the records of \( D \) such that records with identical values on the grouping attributes \( \mathit{G \) are placed into the same group-by bin.}
elements.sent-2411-333: Alongside the original \( n \) columns from \( D \), \( SD \) includes two additional columns: (1) \( \text{start\_index \): Indicates the starting index of each group-by bin in \( D \).}
elements.sent-2411-334: The \( \text{start\_index \) and \( \text{end\_index \) are used for the later aggregation functions such as SUM and others.. {\bf Design.}}}
elements.sent-2411-335: Given an input table $D$ and the group-by attributes $\mathit{G}$, the prover first generates the sorted table $SD$ based on the group-by attributes $\mathit{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-336: This sorting ensures that records with identical values in $\mathit{G}$ are adjacent in $SD$.

elements.sent-2411-338: To identify the starting and ending indices of each group-by bin, we check each record in $SD$ on the group-by attributes $\mathit{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-339: We use a binary value $b$ to indicate whether a record in $SD$ is a starting or ending record, where $1$ signifies that the record is either a starting or ending record.

elements.sent-2411-366: To establish the correctness of the join operations, the prover calculates several types of witnesses locally.
elements.sent-2411-367: Initially, the prover creates two new tables $T'1$ and $T'2$ to reorder the records within them.
elements.sent-2411-368: Specifically, each table $T1'$ and $T2'$ is split into two parts: $T1'_{p}$ and $T2'_{p}$ contain records contributing to the join predicate, while $T1'_{non-p}$ and $T2'_{non-p}$ contain records that do not contribute to the join predicate.
elements.sent-2411-369: The correctness of this reordering is verified using polynomial constraints, as detailed in Equation~\ref{eq:permu2 .}
elements.sent-2411-370: Next, the prover ensures that the records in $T1'$ and $T2'$ indeed contribute to the join predicate.
elements.sent-2411-371: In the context of primary key-foreign key joins (non-primary key-foreign key joins are discussed later), assuming that table T1 contains the foreign key and table T2 contains the primary key, instead of checking the existence of records in one table in another, the prover proves that $T2'_{non-p}$ is disjoint from $T1'_{non-p}$ and $T1'_{p}$.
elements.sent-2411-372: Traditional straight-forward methods involve checking each value in $T1'_{non-p}$ against $T2'_{non-p}$, resulting in a large number of polynomial constraints.
elements.sent-2411-373: To address this, a sorted table $S$ is created to store the unique values in $T1'_{non-p}$ and $T2'_{non-p}$, with a proof of $S_i < S_{i+1}$ for $i \in [0, \text{len (S)-1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-374: However, duplicates~\footnote{Since SQL operates on multisets, duplicates are not only a concern in join algorithms but also throughout the entire query process, as the result must accurately reflect the correct number of duplicates.}
elements.sent-2411-375: In Plookup~\cite{gabizon2020plookup , this can be efficiently managed by encoding the frequency of elements in the multiset into polynomial commitments.}
elements.sent-2411-376: Plookup then ensures that the correct number of occurrences for each value is preserved during query execution, maintaining the semantic integrity of SQL operations.
elements.sent-2411-377: To resolve this, the prover implements a deduplication strategy through the creation of distinct versions, namely $T1^{de}$ and $T2^{de}$.
elements.sent-2411-378: This process ensures that each value in $T1'{non-p}$ is accounted for in $T1^{de}$ and every value in $T2'{non-p}$ is accounted for in $T2^{de}$.
elements.sent-2411-379: To establish this deduplication property, we leverage the mechanism of lookup tables we proposed in Section~\ref{subsec:rangecheck .}
elements.sent-2411-380: The verification of the range check operation, which confirms the existence of each value in a column within a lookup table (i.e. another column), is adapted to ensure the deduplication of $T1^{de}$ and $T2^{de}$.

elements.sent-2411-382: Specifically, $T1^{de}$ and $T2^{de}$ serve as advice columns, signifying the privacy of the data they contain, while a lookup table for range check is stored in an instance column, designating the public nature of the data within it.

elements.sent-2411-432: Since aggregation operations are often applied together with the group-by operation, we describe how to implement them in conjunction with group-by.

elements.sent-2411-437: Furthermore, the MAX and MIN gates are facilitated by a sorting mechanism.

elements.sent-2411-450: We have covered the most common operations used in SQL queries.
elements.sent-2411-451: Other variations of these operations can be constructed using the methods introduced in this work, as long as they can be represented within the circuit framework.
elements.sent-2411-452: PoneglyphDB processes full SQL queries by combining customized gates for different operators as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-453: Mapping Operations to Gates: Each SQL query operation, such as sorting or joining, is represented by a corresponding gate.
elements.sent-2411-454: This gate executes the specific operation, ensuring accurate relationships between inputs and outputs.
elements.sent-2411-455: Predefined Execution Plan and Assembly: The SQL query's predefined execution plan outlines the sequence and dependencies of operations, guiding the assembly of gates in sequence.
elements.sent-2411-456: Each gate's output serves as the input for the next, ensuring data flows correctly through the circuit according to the optimized plan.
elements.sent-2411-457: Combining Gates: Multiple gates are combined to handle various operations as outlined in the execution plan.
elements.sent-2411-458: The gates are strategically assembled in sequence, with the output of one gate serving as the input for the next.
elements.sent-2411-459: Since each operator (such as sorting or aggregation) is verified separately, proving each ensures the correctness of the entire query.
elements.sent-2411-460: However, even when all inputs appear in the output, as with sorting, there is a risk of data leakage from intermediate steps like comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-461: This is because intermediate steps, like comparing the relative order of data elements, may reveal unintended patterns or relationships.
elements.sent-2411-462: This is why we implement oblivious circuits: to ensure that no information about intermediate steps, such as comparisons or data order, is exposed during proof generation.. Correctness.

elements.sent-2411-464: Assume \( G_1 \) receives correctly transformed input and operates correctly, yielding correct output \( O_1 \).
elements.sent-2411-465: Assume for each \( G_i \) (where \( 1 \leq i \leq n \)), the output \( O_i \) is correct and serves as input to \( G_{i+1 \).}
elements.sent-2411-466: Given each gate's correctness, \( G_{i+1 \) operates correctly on \( O_i \) to produce \( O_{i+1 \).}}
elements.sent-2411-467: By induction, the final output \( O_n \) produced by the last gate \( G_n \) is correct.
elements.sent-2411-468: We evaluate \sysname~ in terms of proving and verification time, memory usage, operator performance, proof size and scalability.
elements.sent-2411-469: We implement \sysname's circuits and gates using Halo2, a state-of-the-art ZKP system~\cite{halo2 .}
elements.sent-2411-470: The implementation of all Plonkish circuits for the queries is conducted using Rust.
elements.sent-2411-471: Our evaluation of \sysname~ focuses on a selected subset of the TPC-H benchmark~\cite{TPC-H2023 , specifically targeting queries that are representative of common data analytics workloads.}
elements.sent-2411-472: We compare with ZKSQL~\cite{li2023zksql , a state-of-the-art solution for using interactive ZKP in database systems.}
elements.sent-2411-473: The interactivity in ZKSQL involves breaking down the computation into smaller sub-circuits to reduce the complexity of the overall circuit.
elements.sent-2411-474: These sub-circuits are then verified interactively, where the prover demonstrates the correctness of each sub-circuit step-by-step.
elements.sent-2411-475: The interaction ensures that the combined outputs of the sub-circuits correspond to the correct execution of the SQL query, allowing the verifier to check the correctness of the entire query process incrementally.
elements.sent-2411-476: However, this interactivity increases the communication and computational overhead, as each round of interaction requires multiple exchanges between the prover and verifier.
elements.sent-2411-477: To align with existing research evaluations of ZKP-based databases and to conduct a fair comparison with ZKSQL, we have implemented the six TPC-H queries identified in their evaluation: Q1, Q3, Q5, Q8, Q9, and Q18.
elements.sent-2411-478: In addition, we compare our system with Libra~\cite{xie2019libra , a state-of-the-art non-interactive ZKP system that leverages the GKR protocol~\cite{goldwasser2015delegating , which is also foundational to vSQL~\cite{zhang2017vsql .}}}
elements.sent-2411-479: To the best of our knowledge, Libra is the most efficient publicly available system utilizing the GKR protocol.
elements.sent-2411-480: Since the high-level logic for implementing SQL operations in vSQL can be adapted across various ZKP systems, we use Libra’s circuit structure to implement SQL operations based on the logic and optimizations introduced in vSQL.
elements.sent-2411-481: While Libra uses a fixed input structure, we adopt an alternative approach to avoid the need for relay gates for inputs that may not be needed immediately.
elements.sent-2411-482: That is, we split the circuit into multiple parts, ensuring that each part includes only the necessary inputs in its input layer.

elements.sent-2411-509: In Libra, complex operations such as sorting require a large number of basic gates, with each gate limited to two inputs, which increases both the circuit depth and size.
elements.sent-2411-510: For comparison operations in SQL queries, decimal values are represented using full 64-bit binary representations in Libra.
elements.sent-2411-511: Logical operations on these 64-bit binary numbers necessitate circuits that handle each bit individually, including managing carry bits across the entire bit width.

elements.sent-2411-514: In contrast, \sysname~ optimizes the handling of decimal values by segmenting them into 8-bit chunks and leveraging lookup tables to efficiently validate and perform operations on each segment.

elements.sent-2411-518: We selected these two queries for our performance evaluation because they encompass a comprehensive range of SQL operations, including multiple aggregations, joins, group-by, sort, and filtering functions.
elements.sent-2411-519: The proof generation process begins with the construction of a comprehensive circuit, encapsulating all facets of witness generation; this preliminary phase is described as a ``circuit without any gates''.
elements.sent-2411-520: Following this, the procedure advances to the integration of polynomial constraints—or gates—that correspond to the SQL operations.
elements.sent-2411-521: These operations include filtering, grouping by, ordering, and performing eight aggregations for query Q1, as well as applying three filters, executing two joins, a group-by, an order-by, and an aggregation for query Q3.
elements.sent-2411-522: The results show that the initial step takes over 50 seconds, attributable to the fixed overheads determined by the chosen public parameter; a larger public parameter size increases this initial step overhead.
elements.sent-2411-523: The significant overhead in proof generation, notably from the aggregations in Q1 and the filters and joins in Q3, can be attributed to the extensive computational resources required.
elements.sent-2411-524: Aggregation operations, for instance, necessitate the collation and computation across sizable datasets to yield a singular summary outcome.
elements.sent-2411-525: This task demands multiple iterations of data processing and polynomial constraints verification within the circuit, thereby amplifying its complexity and extending the duration needed for proof generation.
elements.sent-2411-526: Similarly, filters and joins in Q3 are computationally intensive, as filtering requires checking each record against conditions, while joins involve aligning records from different tables based on join keys.

elements.figure-2411-013: \centerline{\psfig{figure=pics/expe/scale.pdf, width=0.49\textwidth} } \captionsetup{skip=5pt} \caption{Proof generation time and memory usage over data of increasing sizes.} \label{fig:scale}
elements.sent-2411-527: We evaluate the scalibility of \sysname~ by generating proofs with larger workloads.
elements.sent-2411-528: We evaluate with the workload of TPC-H’s lineitem table at 120k and 240k rows as the lineitem table dominates the complexity of the SQL queries.
elements.sent-2411-529: As depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:scale , the running time and memory consumption increases with the increase in the size of the dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-530: Specifically, the running times for the six SQL queries---Q1, Q3, Q5, Q8, Q9, and Q18---exhibit a gradual increase as the database size increases from 60k to 240k Rows.
elements.sent-2411-531: The running time for query Q1 starts at 180 seconds for 60k rows and increases to 683 seconds for the 240k row dataset, indicating a proportional relationship between dataset size and observed performance.
elements.sent-2411-532: This is because the size of our circuits linearly grows with the size of inputs and all the polynomial constraints enforced on the circuits have low degrees.
elements.sent-2411-533: This validates our design goal of maintaining low-degree polynomials in {\sysname 's circuits.}
elements.sent-2411-534: Memory usage shows a similar pattern, with the memory footprint for query Q1 starting at 1.53 GB for 60k rows and increasing to 5.12 GB for 240k rows.
elements.sent-2411-535: There is substantial research into verifiable SQL querying, employing a variety of techniques that ensure the integrity and security of query results.
elements.sent-2411-536: These methods can be broadly categorized into three groups: Authenticated Data Structures (ADS)~\cite{tamassia2003authenticated , Trusted Execution Environments (TEE)~\cite{sabt2015trusted , and Cryptographic Proof Techniques~\cite{fiege1987zero .}}}
elements.sent-2411-537: ADS-based methods use asymmetric cryptography to authenticate data, requiring extra memory to maintain authenticated data structures for SQL query verification \cite{bajaj2013correctdb, yang2009authenticated, zheng2012efficient, papadopoulos2015practical, papadopoulos2014taking .}
elements.sent-2411-538: While secure, these methods are generally limited to specific computational tasks.
elements.sent-2411-539: TEE-based approaches, exemplified by \cite{zhou2021veridb, zheng2017opaque, sinha2018veritasdb, bajaj2013correctdb, arasu2017concerto , secure SQL query results through computations performed within trusted hardware environments.}
elements.sent-2411-540: Basic TEE implementations might expose sensitive data through program traces.
elements.sent-2411-541: Integrating TEE with Oblivious Random Access Machine (ORAM), as in \cite{goodrich2012privacy , can obscure such traces but at the cost of additional computation time, highlighted in \cite{le2020tale, alam2023sgx .}}
elements.sent-2411-542: Cryptographic proof techniques, such as zk-SNARKs~\cite{ben2013snarks and zk-STARKs~\cite{ben2019scalable , enable entities to verify the correctness of computations without revealing underlying data.}}
elements.sent-2411-543: These techniques have found widespread application across various domains, including blockchain off-chain computations and privacy-preserving machine learning~\cite{zhu2023secure, gu2024zk, lin2024rollstore, gu2023ml .}
elements.sent-2411-544: These techniques ensure high levels of security but are resource-intensive, requiring substantial memory to manage numerous intermediate values and considerable time to create proofs.
elements.sent-2411-545: Prior cryptographic proof systems, such as IntegriDB~\cite{zhang2015integridb and vSQL~\cite{zhang2017vsql , employ cryptographic verifiable computation to validate a wide range of SQL queries.}}
elements.sent-2411-546: While IntegriDB and vSQL ensure data integrity, they operate in an outsourcing model and do not inherently provide zero-knowledge properties.
elements.sent-2411-547: An extension of vSQL, referred to as vSQL+~\cite{zhang2017zero , introduces ZKP; however, it lacks support for ad-hoc queries and does not thoroughly address practical efficiency or the translation of arbitrary SQL statements into cryptographic protocols necessary for such guarantees.}
elements.sent-2411-548: Notably, vSQL and vSQL+ are based on public-coin protocols~\cite{ZK-origins, goldwasser1986private , which can be transformed into non-interactive ZKP systems using the Fiat-Shamir heuristic~\cite{fiat1986prove .}}
elements.sent-2411-549: ZKSQL~\cite{li2023zksql reduces the proving cost by dividing the entire circuit into smaller sub-circuits to reduce the size of the overall circuit.}
elements.sent-2411-550: This approach supports ad-hoc queries and maintains zero-knowledge properties, but it shares the common limitations of interactive ZKP.
elements.sent-2411-551: ZKSQL is based on designated-verifier protocols~\cite{goldwasser1986private , where the Fiat-Shamir heuristic cannot generally be applied to transform the protocol into a non-interactive proof.}
elements.sent-2411-552: Our system, \sysname, generates non-interactive ZKP using recursive proof composition \cite{ben2017scalable, bunz2020recursive, bowe2019recursive, kothapalli2022nova .}
elements.sent-2411-553: It enhances proof generation performance by optimizing arithmetic circuits.
elements.sent-2411-554: We introduce {\sysname , a non-interactive ZKP-based database designed for efficient confidentiality and provability.}
